title: LIU vs Arizona — First Round Viewing Guide
eventTag: NCAA Tournament • First Round • San Diego, CA
venue: Viejas Arena, San Diego, California • TNT
extraInfo: Winner faces #8 Oregon or #9 Clemson on Sunday

stakes.callout: A Brooklyn school coached by a former NBA point guard with nearly 8,000 career assists against the #1 overall seed with three potential NBA lottery picks. Arizona is 32-2, won the Big 12 regular season and tournament, and has the best freshman class in America. LIU is making its first NCAA Tournament appearance since 2018 and hasn't won a tournament game since 1939. A 16-seed has beaten a 1-seed exactly twice in history. This would be the third.

cards.label: For LIU
cards.value: First NCAA Tournament since 2018. First as the "Sharks" (after the LIU Brooklyn/LIU Post merger). The program's last tournament win was in the 1939 NIT — 87 years ago. Rod Strickland has transformed this program from afterthought to NEC champions in four seasons. Just being here is the story.

cards.label: For Arizona
cards.value: This is a national championship team. 32-2 with the Big 12 double crown. Three freshman starters who are all projected NBA Draft picks. Tommy Lloyd has the most wins in NCAA history through five seasons. The 1-seed doesn't lose this game — they survive it and move on to chase the program's first title since 1997.

cards.label: The 16-vs-1 History
cards.value: A 16-seed has beaten a 1-seed exactly twice in 152 opportunities: UMBC over Virginia in 2018 and Fairleigh Dickinson over Purdue in 2023. Both times, the 1-seed had a clear weakness that was exploited. Arizona doesn't appear to have one — they're elite on both ends.

cards.label: The Strickland Factor
cards.value: Rod Strickland played 17 NBA seasons, scored 14,000 points, and dished nearly 8,000 assists. He's a New York City Basketball Hall of Famer. This is his first head coaching job. Coaching against the #1 seed in his first tournament is the kind of moment his entire second career has been building toward.

styles.title: Brooklyn Defense, NBA Pedigree
bullets: Defense wins NEC championships: LIU ranks 109th in KenPom defensive efficiency (104.0) — strong for a 16-seed. They allow just 71.1 PPG. Greg Gordon (NEC Defensive Player of the Year) anchors a system built on Strickland's NBA experience.

Balanced scoring: Three players average 14+ PPG — Fuller (16.4), Davis (14.4), Gordon (14.1). No one player dominates, which makes them hard to game-plan for within the NEC. The question is whether those numbers translate against Big 12-level defenders.
Efficient shooting: 48.1% from the field and 36.1% from three as a team. Fuller shoots 43.8% from three on volume. They won't shoot themselves out of the game with careless possessions.

Size mismatch: LIU's tallest rotation player is Isaiah Miranda at 7-1, but he only averages 4.0 PPG and 1.5 RPG. Arizona starts a 7-2 center (Krivas) and has two 6-8 forwards (Peat, Awaka). The interior matchup is a massive problem.

Weakness: Offensive efficiency ranks 210th nationally. They average 74.1 PPG against NEC competition. Arizona allows 68.8 PPG against the Big 12. The math doesn't work unless LIU plays the best game of their lives.

styles.title: Three Lottery Picks and a 7-Footer
bullets: Historically great team: Arizona is 32-2 with the #2 KenPom ranking. They rank 17th in offensive efficiency and 9th in defensive efficiency. They opened with 23 straight wins and were unanimous #1 in the AP poll for four straight weeks. This is one of the best teams in the country by every measure.

The freshman trio: Brayden Burries (15.9 PPG), Koa Peat (13.6), and Ivan Kharchenkov (10.1) have combined for 99 starts. All three are projected NBA Draft picks. Burries is a projected top-10 pick. Peat scored 30 in his college debut against defending champion Florida.

Krivas controls the paint: Motiejus Krivas is 7-2, 260 pounds, averaging 10.5 PPG, 8.1 RPG, and 1.8 BPG. He's First-Team All-Big 12, All-Defensive Team, and a first-round NBA Draft prospect. LIU has no one who can match him physically.

Bradley is the veteran glue: Jaden Bradley (13.3 PPG, 4.5 APG, .404 3P%) is the Big 12 Player of the Year and the lone senior among the starters. A former five-star recruit who transferred from Alabama, he's the steady hand guiding three elite freshmen.

Weakness: Freshmen in March. Arizona relies on three freshmen more than any recent 1-seed. The tournament is a different beast — new arenas, hostile crowds, win-or-go-home pressure. Peat shoots just 59.7% from the free throw line, which could matter late in a closer-than-expected game.

keys.title: Can LIU survive the first 10 minutes?
keys.description: Arizona's talent advantage is enormous. If the Wildcats jump out to a 20-point lead early, this game is over before halftime. LIU's best chance is to keep it competitive through the first 10 minutes — make Arizona feel some pressure, force them to think. Every 16-over-1 upset started with the underdog refusing to be intimidated early. Strickland's NBA calm on the sideline matters here.

keys.title: The interior mismatch.
keys.description: Motiejus Krivas (7-2, 260 lbs, 10.5 PPG, 8.1 RPG) and Tobe Awaka (6-8, 9.5 RPG) against LIU's frontcourt is a colossal mismatch. Shadrak Lasu (6-9) and Isaiah Miranda (7-1) are LIU's only players with real size, but neither averages more than 5.4 RPG. If Arizona pounds the ball inside, LIU has no answer. LIU needs to make this a perimeter game.

keys.title: Turnovers and transition.
keys.description: LIU's defense forces turnovers. Arizona's offense thrives in transition. If LIU's aggressive defense creates steals but Arizona gets out in transition off the misses, it could backfire. LIU needs to force turnovers that lead to half-court possessions, not fast breaks. Disciplined pressure, not gambling.

keys.title: Jamal Fuller as an X-factor.
keys.description: Fuller shoots 43.8% from three and averages 16.4 PPG. If he catches fire from deep, LIU can hang around. Arizona's perimeter defense is good but not impenetrable (they allow 36% from three). A Fuller eruption is the only realistic path to an upset — the kind of individual performance that makes a 16-seed dangerous for one afternoon.

keys.title: Arizona's freshman nerves.
keys.description: Burries, Peat, and Kharchenkov are all freshmen in their first NCAA Tournament game. They've been dominant all year, but the tournament is different. UMBC beat Virginia in 2018 partly because Virginia's players tightened up under the 1-seed pressure. If Arizona's freshmen play tight in the first half, LIU's veterans could capitalize. It's unlikely — but it's the only door.

prediction.summary: Let's be honest: Arizona is going to win this game. The Wildcats are 32-2, ranked 2nd in KenPom, and have three freshman lottery picks plus a 7-2 center. LIU's offensive efficiency ranks 210th — against the NEC. Arizona allows 68.8 PPG against Big 12 competition. The math simply doesn't work. The only question is whether LIU can keep it competitive enough to make Rod Strickland's first tournament as a coach a proud moment, and whether Jamal Fuller can put on a show from deep.

phases.title: Opening 10 Minutes
phases.description: Arizona's athleticism and length are immediately apparent. Burries and Peat attack in transition, and Krivas (7-2, 260) establishes position inside against LIU's overmatched frontcourt. But Fuller hits an early three and Gordon's two-way energy keeps LIU engaged. Arizona leads by 8-10 but LIU hasn't folded.

phases.title: Mid-First Half
phases.description: Arizona goes on a run. Bradley orchestrates the offense with his 4.5 APG and .404 three-point accuracy, finding Peat on back-cuts and Burries in transition. LIU's defense, respectable at 109th in KenPom, can't contain Arizona's movement and the lead balloons to 15-18. Strickland calls a timeout to settle his team.

phases.title: Second Half Start
phases.description: LIU comes out with nothing to lose. Fuller launches from deep and hits a couple, and Davis creates off the dribble for a mini-run. The Sharks cut it to 14 for a moment. But Arizona's depth is crushing — Kharchenkov, Awaka, and Tobe off the bench are better than most of LIU's starters. The Wildcats re-establish control.

phases.title: Final 10 Minutes
phases.description: Arizona empties the bench with a comfortable lead. The freshman trio — Burries, Peat, and Kharchenkov — all finish in double figures. LIU's players soak in the moment, knowing they earned their way here. Strickland shakes Tommy Lloyd's hand knowing his 17 NBA seasons prepared him for this coaching journey. Arizona advances easily.

projectedScore.rationale: Arizona's talent advantage is simply too large. Krivas dominates inside, the freshman trio scores at will, and LIU's NEC-level offense can't generate enough against the 9th-ranked defense in America.

players.name: Jamal Fuller
players.bio: From Toronto, Canada. In his second year at LIU. Leads the team in scoring and three-point makes (51). Shoots 43.8% from three on volume — LIU's most likely candidate for a breakout performance. First-Team All-NEC after earning Second-Team honors last season. If LIU is going to hang around, Fuller needs to be unconscious from deep.

players.name: Greg Gordon
players.meta: G • 6-5 • Senior • NEC Tournament MVP, NEC Defensive Player of the Year
players.bio: The heart of this team. Named NEC Tournament MVP after scoring 24 points in the championship game. Also the NEC Defensive Player of the Year — ranked top 10 in the NEC in points, rebounds, steals, and blocks. Shoots 56.5% from the field. The rare two-way mid-major star.

players.name: Malachi Davis
players.bio: Senior guard who matched Gordon with 24 points in the NEC championship game against Mercyhurst. A versatile scorer who can create off the dribble — a skill honed under the tutelage of a coach who dished nearly 8,000 NBA assists. Davis will need the game of his life against Arizona's perimeter defenders.

players.name: Shadrak Lasu
players.bio: Sophomore forward who earned First-Team All-NEC honors. At 6-9, he's LIU's best hope of contesting Krivas in the paint. Shoots a surprising 47.1% from three on limited attempts. Will need to play the biggest game of his young career just to keep Arizona's interior scoring in check.

players.name: Brayden Burries
players.meta: G • 6-4 • Freshman • Big 12 Newcomer of the Year
players.bio:
Five-star from Eastvale, California. #12 in the ESPN 100. Led Eleanor Roosevelt HS to a California state championship, scoring 30 PPG. Projected top-10 pick in the 2026 NBA Draft. Named AP Player of the Week during the season. Played through bronchitis to help clinch the Big 12 title. The leading scorer on a 32-2 team — as a freshman.
Read more →

players.name: Koa Peat
players.meta: F • 6-8 • Freshman • Four-Time USA Basketball Gold Medalist
players.bio:
Five-star from Gilbert, Arizona. His name means "strong warrior" in Hawaiian (Native Hawaiian descent). Son of former NFL guard Todd Peat (Cardinals/Raiders). Youngest of seven children. First player in USA Basketball history to win four junior international gold medals. Committed to Arizona on The Pat McAfee Show. Scored 30 in his college debut against defending champion Florida. Projected lottery pick.
Read more →

players.name: Jaden Bradley
players.meta: G • 6-3 • Senior • Big 12 Player of the Year
players.bio:
From Rochester, NY. Five-star who committed to Alabama, started as a freshman, fell out of the rotation, then transferred to Arizona. Spent Year 1 as a reserve. Became a starter in Year 2. Now in Year 3, he's the Big 12 Player of the Year averaging 13.3 points and 4.5 assists. The veteran guiding three elite freshmen through their first March.
Read more →

players.name: Motiejus Krivas
players.meta: C • 7-2 • Junior • First-Team All-Big 12, All-Defensive Team
players.bio:
From Siauliai, Lithuania. Recruited through Tommy Lloyd's Gonzaga international pipeline. At 7-2, 260 pounds, he's one of the most imposing centers in college basketball. First-Team All-Big 12 and All-Defensive Team. Legitimate first-round NBA Draft prospect. The rim protector who makes Arizona's top-10 defense possible.
Read more →

funFacts.title: The Brooklyn Paramount (LIU)
funFacts.body: LIU's campus is built around the Brooklyn Paramount Theater, purchased in 1960. The theater hosted Dizzy Gillespie, Ella Fitzgerald, Miles Davis, Duke Ellington, Chuck Berry, and Buddy Holly. LIU's basketball court sits in a building where jazz and rock 'n' roll were born.

funFacts.title: Three No. 1 Seeds in One Tournament (Arizona)
funFacts.body: In 1997, Arizona became the only team ever to beat three No. 1 seeds in a single NCAA Tournament — Kansas, North Carolina, and Kentucky (in OT in the championship). Miles Simon was the Final Four MVP. The team had no seniors among its top eight players.

funFacts.title: 14,000 Points and 8,000 Assists (LIU)
funFacts.body: LIU coach Rod Strickland played 17 NBA seasons, scoring over 14,000 points and recording nearly 8,000 assists. He ranks 13th all-time in NBA career assists. He was drafted 19th overall by the Knicks in 1988 and is in the NYC Basketball Hall of Fame.

funFacts.title: "Strong Warrior" (Arizona)
funFacts.body: Koa Peat's name means "strong warrior" in Hawaiian. He's of Native Hawaiian descent through his mother. His father Todd played nine NFL seasons. Koa is the youngest of seven children — and the first player in USA Basketball history to win four junior international gold medals.

funFacts.title: Ray Dalio Went Here (LIU)
funFacts.body: LIU's most powerful alumnus is Ray Dalio, founder of Bridgewater Associates, one of the world's largest hedge funds. Other notable alumni include actress Rosie Perez and actor John Amos (*Good Times*, *Roots*).

funFacts.title: 52 Points in a Game (Arizona)
funFacts.body: Before he was a coach, Tommy Lloyd scored 52 points in a single game at Walla Walla Community College — still the school record. He also played professionally in Australia and Germany. Now he has the most wins in NCAA history through five seasons as a head coach.

funFacts.title: The 1951 Scandal (LIU)
funFacts.body: LIU's basketball program was suspended for six years (1951-57) after players were caught in the infamous point-shaving scandal. Sherman White, named Sporting News Player of the Year the day before his arrest, was among those involved. The program has been rebuilding its reputation ever since.

funFacts.title: The Lithuanian Giant (Arizona)
funFacts.body: Motiejus Krivas is 7-2, 260 pounds from Siauliai, Lithuania. He was recruited through Tommy Lloyd's Gonzaga international pipeline and has become a first-round NBA Draft prospect and First-Team All-Big 12 center.

funFacts.title: The Pat McAfee Commitment (Arizona)
funFacts.body: Koa Peat announced his commitment to Arizona live on The Pat McAfee Show on ESPN. His high school (Perry HS in Gilbert, AZ) won four straight Arizona state titles — including three consecutive AIA Open Division championships.

funFacts.title: From Alabama Benchwarmer to Big 12 POY (Arizona)
funFacts.body: Jaden Bradley was a five-star recruit who committed to Alabama, started as a freshman, then fell out of the lineup. He transferred to Arizona and spent his first year as a reserve. Now in Year 3, he's the Big 12 Player of the Year. Patience and growth, personified.

funFacts.title: From Blackbirds to Sharks (LIU)
funFacts.body: When LIU Brooklyn and LIU Post merged their athletics programs in 2019, they became the "Sharks." This is the first NCAA Tournament appearance in the Sharks era — and the program's first since 2018 when they were still the LIU Brooklyn Blackbirds.

funFacts.title: 30 Points in His Debut (Arizona)
funFacts.body: Koa Peat scored 30 points in his college debut — a nationally televised upset of defending champion #3 Florida. It was a statement game that announced Arizona as a national title contender from Day 1.

history.callout: Arizona has a national championship (1997), 35 NCAA Tournament appearances, and the #1 seed this year. LIU won the NIT in 1939 and 1941 under legendary coach Clair Bee — then was decimated by a point-shaving scandal that suspended the program for six years. This is a 1-vs-16 matchup between a national title contender and a program that is just happy to be dancing.

timeline.text: Lute Olson takes Arizona to the Final Four for the first time, establishing the program as a national power.
Arizona wins the national championship, beating three No. 1 seeds (Kansas, North Carolina, Kentucky in OT). The only team ever to accomplish this.
Arizona returns to the championship game, losing to Duke. The program's second Final Four in five years.
Tommy Lloyd hired from Gonzaga. Sets the record for most wins in NCAA history through his first five seasons.
32-2. Big 12 double champions. #1 seed. Three freshman lottery picks. Sporting News Coach of the Year. Chasing the program's second championship.

subsections.title: LIU: From NIT Glory to Scandal to Redemption
subsections.content:
Long Island University was once a basketball powerhouse. Under coach Clair Bee, the Blackbirds won the NIT in 1939 (finishing 24-0 with a 43-game winning streak) and again in 1941. The NIT was the premier postseason tournament at the time — more prestigious than the then-fledgling NCAA Tournament.

Then came 1951. Three LIU players were caught in a massive point-shaving scandal that also engulfed CCNY, Kentucky, and others. Sherman White — named Sporting News Player of the Year the day before his arrest — admitted to taking money to fix games. The basketball program was suspended for six years.

The program never fully recovered. LIU has made the NCAA Tournament seven times since the suspension ended, but has never won a game in the Big Dance. In 2019, LIU Brooklyn and LIU Post merged their athletics, becoming the "Sharks." Now, under Rod Strickland — an NBA legend making his first tournament appearance as a coach — the Sharks are dancing for the first time. Win or lose, this is a story about a program finding its way back from the darkest chapter in college basketball history.

subsections.title: The 16-vs-1 Upsets: Could It Happen Again?
subsections.content:
In 152 games between 1-seeds and 16-seeds, the underdog has won exactly twice. In 2018, UMBC crushed Virginia 74-54 — not just an upset, a 20-point blowout. Virginia's pack-line defense was exploited by three-point shooting, and the Cavaliers tightened up under pressure. In 2023, Fairleigh Dickinson beat Purdue 63-58 — FDU's zone defense confused Purdue's offense, and the Boilermakers' Zach Edey couldn't impose his will.

Could LIU be the third? The honest answer: it would take a miracle. Arizona is 32-2, elite on both ends, and deeper than either Virginia or Purdue were. But Rod Strickland has played in 1,094 NBA games — he knows what pressure looks like. And every upset starts with someone refusing to accept they're supposed to lose.
